turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear- arguments next in Robert Howe v. Attorney- General. Mr. Nelson, I think you may proceed whenever you're ready.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The petitioner is a Vermont convict who is in prison, for him?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Well, I think that the 2nd Circuit's view is reasonable, that his incarceration is not for treatment at all but simply for deterrence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think -- in other words, you think that it must be treatment of some sort?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Yes. We feel that the statute permits ment .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why do you make it any different when it's in federal custody as a surrogate for the state, than when he is in the state custody with the state doing it on its own behalf?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Well, a prisoner committed to state custo dy is simply committed to the Commissioner of Corrections or to the prison system for whatever purpose imprisonment may serve. The same is true of a prisoner' committed' to federal prison. '.He is simply committed to a penal or correctional institution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is, after a federal criminal trial. It says, "Custody, care, subsistence, education, treatment, and training," doesn't it?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It doesn't, it isn't limited to'treatment?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: No, it's not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I was waiting for you to say it.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, couldn't that be facilities for treatment?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Well, that's not the Government's posi­ tion and it's not the state's position, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Answer my question; it's my question.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: I don't think that the Bureau --no, I don't think it could be construed as that. The Bureau did not intend it as a certification, specifically, treatment facilia ties; simply, facilities generally. there are treatment facilities available in the federal prison system. Our view is supported by the construction placed on this statute by the House committee which passed it. It said that the section was limited to inmates with treatment needs. In the Senate the same view was expressed, although perhaps not quite so clearly. Senator McCarran,. asked What'.the bill would accomplish, said it would permit the transfer of state inmates of cases. Neither of those sources indicates that this statute

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Some food.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if you invite him for custody?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Inmates here are not invited for custody. That's -- they're invited for treatment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but the reading of the statute, it seems to me, it's particularly phrased in the -- with the "and" as it is, would include custody as a separate facet.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: All these are incidentals, necessary incidentals to -- Corrections Act. It's clear that 5003(a) was modeled after 512, in part, after 512 of the Youth Corrections Act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Nelson, it's entirely a different ques­ tion. I just want to get something straight in my mind. You don't raise any challenge, do you, to the power of the Vermont authorities to enter into contracts for the placement of people in their custody elsewhere?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: No, this case solely concerns the power a temporary period? HR. NELSON: No, I don't think that’s arguable. It may be arguable that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But in the first question about Vermont and New Hampshire, it seems to me you're suggesting you could not challenge New Hampshire's custody.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You assert a right under 18 U.S.C. 4001(a), don't you?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: That’s correct. Federal Government.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could the Federal Government release him? MR. NELSON Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could the Federal Government release him? MR. NELSON No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So who is he in the custody of?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Except on the say-so of Vermont.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whose custody is that, now? Whose custody is he in? does, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that visitation is a possible considera­ tion?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Visitation is a major consideration. Visitation with family and friends, visitation with counsel. I have never met Mr. Howe. Another consideration, of course, is the parole possibilities. Mr. Howe is sentenced to life impri­ sonment. Life imprisonment in Vermont is actually zero to life. He is parolable, technically, right now. It's difficult if not

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Under constitutional restraints, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's why you focus on the power of the Federal Government to receive rather than the power of the Ver­ mont court to transfer?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: That's correct. We see a clear right in federal law against federal imprisonment which is not authorized by statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What can 5003 mean if it doen't mean that want to talk about the core of it, is that the Federal Govern­ ment can make available, Congress has said the Federal Govern­ state prisoners.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: That is -- well, one independent clause says --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well., why do you have to" parse the .particu­ lar words in order to discern that meaning?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: It may do that, it -may make federal pri­

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Oh, I think the words "treatment facilities" in common speech means, among other things, mental hospitals or hospitals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It may mean that to you but it doesn't mean that necessarily to everyone.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: No, perhaps not. I'm not saying that it means --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Adequate;treatment means, do they have the space, do they have the'facilities to take oh more inmates? That it's no

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any treatment In the Vermont prisons that's not available in the federal prisons?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: The record seems to say, no, that the facilities are about equivalent in both places.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Yes, that the same types -- commitment different from ordinary prison; therapeutic.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you suggesting, do I take the argument to mean that this institution does not have a medical unit, for example, to deal with people who become ill, or that they have no facilities to deal with a person who has alcoholic tendencies when he arrives, although surely he couldn’t become an alco­ holic after he got there?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Perhaps.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Perhaps. you said earlier, not the authority of Vermont- to send him but the authority of the Federal Government, to receive.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: ' That's correct. . QUESTION: - Where is your -standing, your client's standing to challenge'what the Federal, Government is doing,- even though it's holding him in custody?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Our stand -- we assert, standing under 4001, Title 18. "No citizen may be confined by the United States except pursuant to an act of Congress."

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: It certainly is, but we say it doesn't cover this case. As we construe it, it does not authorize the transfer of the prisoner to be warehoused.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Solely because of the word "treatment?"

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: No. Solely because of the phrase, "treatment facilities and personnel."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You told me, I said, if we took the word "treatment" out would you be out? And you said, yes.

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: I said, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you have changed. Now what's your treatment that is available in the federal prison system. It is the position of the United States and the State of Vermont that Section 5003 is not so limited. Rather', in our view, the statutory requirements are met and any state prisoner regardless of treatment needs may be transferred from state to federal custody once a contract has been entered into between the state and federal governments, and once the Director of the Federal institutions are available. On behalf of the United States I family to visit him, yet he would assert no challenge to that type of transfer. There is no suggestion in the statutory language that the federal prison system's authority to assume custody of a state prisoner is conditioned on the prisoner's need for spe­ cialized treatment available in the federal system. The plain language of Section 5003(a), as the Court recognizes, author­ izes the Attorney General to contract not only for the purpose of treatment but equally for the purposes of custody, care, facilities" were intended to refer to something narrower than the typical facilities that we provide for all federal prison­ ers, Section 5003 still requires no more than a certification that facilities are available, presumably in the case that the prisoner would need them. But nothing in the'statutory language suggests that the Director must make an individualized determi^- nation in the case of each potential state transferee that he needs specialized treatment available in the federal system and that specific treatment facilities have been earmarked for

turns.name: ETKIND
turns.text: Right. And while we would assert that there is no private right of action, we have not pressed that because most of these cases have come up under habeas and there certainly would be a right if the petitioner is right in the way he construes the statute, then there would be a remedy

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's your normal remedy in habeas? Congress a remedy. What else would 4001(a) do?

turns.name: ETKIND
turns.text: Well, the legislative history of 4001(a) is very specific. That statute was passed in connection with the repeal of the emergency, the Internal Security Act, a provision that allowed citizens to be rounded up and detained if there was just probable cause to believe that they were, engaged in some sort of illegal activity without conviction. And the legislative history makes quite clear that 4001 was enacted in order to provide that citizens would not have to be does it confer a benefit on? Is there a special class of per­ sons? And there sure is a special class defined in 4001(a), persons who are imprisoned except pursuant to an act of Congress imprisoned by the United States without any act of Congress au­ thorizing it. that the persons for whomithey were concerned and to'whom they wanted / examined and classified, and if they were found to have particu­ lar problems, for example, alcoholism and emotional disorders, then the statute would provide in that state, shall receive treatment for such condition. Now, could that prisoner be transferred to a federal institution absent a certification by someone that treatment was available? In other words, absent that situation, they would not be complying with th’e state

turns.name: ETKIND
turns.text: That's a much closer question and I think determined to make that available. But in a gross sense the intent of Congress was not to limit it in that way, it was just to make what we had available to the states. Although we view the statutory language as unambiguous resort to the legislative history demonstrates, as I said, that the intent was to match the states' needs with federal surpluses The petitioner’s construction would frustrate that intent by preventing the Federal Government, even when it has the neces­ sary surpluses, from meeting the urgent needs of the state cor­ regardless of how much he could benefit from or indeed desires certain aspects of federal incarceration, unless such a prisoner was otherwise in need of specialized treatment available in the federal system. As the court below put it, adopting petitioner's view of the statute would mean that a wholly incorrigible prisoner or on the other hand a fully rehabilitated prisoner incarcerated not for treatment but solely for purposes of deterrence could.not be transferred from state to federal custody solely to take advantage of training and educational opportunities available only at federal facilities. faciliries for its offenders. This Court is well aware of the severe overcrowding problems in state and local penal institu­ tions generally. As of mid-December, 1980, however --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I interrupt you, just to go back to that question I asked before? Prior to' "the enactment' of 5003, did the Federal Government ever take custody of state prisoners, to your knowledge?

turns.name: ETKIND
turns.text: I don't know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So apparently the states did find some other way to solve these emergency problems, because I assume there were problems from time to time where state facilities developing special programs such as for alcoholics and drug addicts and youth corrections and the like.

turns.name: ETKIND
turns.text: Oh, we had that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Contemporaneously with the enactment of this statute.

turns.name: ETKIND
turns.text: Well, the legislative history doesn't indicate that we were developing them at that time. I think it indicates and I think it's clear that we had them at those times. As of mid-December, 1980, the entire federal system facilities are not available. We submit that considerable deference is due such a contemporaneous and consistent interpretation of the statute by the agency that drafted the legislation, suggested its enact­ ment to Congress, and has been charged with its administration ever since. I'd like to rely on our arguments in our brief with from the Court. Thank you. decided to close down the maximum security prison, when we were spending a full 25 percent of our resources on two percent of those incarcerated? I think that's really the issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Easton, I take it nobody disputes the statement in the opinion of the Court of Appeals for the 2nd facilities, facilities just not equipped to deal with prisoners such as Mr. Howe.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But New Hampshire, for example, I think, has a maximum security institution.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: That it does, and we have a compact with them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, doesn't that extend also to some of your educational facilities? Doesn't Vermont have an agreement with Maine that Vermont law students can go to Maine, the community. We're talking, in a state like Vermont, of a very small correctional system. We have less than 500 prisoners. We have 3,700 probationers.'. Our whole corrections population is smaller than the size of several state institutions. Our system consists of six facilities around the state, four commu­ nity correction centers and two state-wide centers. The largest only has a capacity of less than 150. All the rest are under 100. These are --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose the legal question is, say we system unless the federal system offers something that Vermont does not offer in the way of special treatment.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: Yes, I think the court below found --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Federal.Government' isn't just going into the business of making their facilities available on a wholesale basis.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: As the court below found, Justice Stevens, even if some of the same activities and programs were available in Vermont, they're less accessible to Mr. Howe because of his

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I take it that your friend's position would be the same if the federal institution to which the trans­ fer was made were 200 yards away from your closed institution in Vermont. Do you assume that that's the case?

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: I do, Your Honor, and also --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It isn't the out-of-state factor, it's the transfer to a federal institution. to be in Vermont, but the statute which he challenges -- .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the claim certainly is that there's a statutory right not to be in the custody of the Federal Government. Their claim is that the statute forbids custody of this prisoner by the Federal Government.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: Perhaps I stated it wrong. Let me say, Justice White, that the issue is whether there is any entitle­ ment, despite the claim, whether the statute gives him any entitlement. And we suggest that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you can call it what you want to, but he has an entitlement?

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: He has to have some basis on which to challenge his custody, and he's challenging the basis in the statute under 5003.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, his challenge is that laws of the United States expressly say you can't do this, you cannot deliver me to a federal authority.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: He's attacking 5003 and his attack on 5003 -- treatment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, it's the Government that's relying on 5003 .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, he is.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: Well, I think Justice White, in his ques­ tion that he presents, that he himself is saying that there's got to be a finding under 5003.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought his entire argument was focusing on the term "treatment" in 5003(a). even be in the case.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: That's true. He's entitled --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And saying that, basing it on 4001 saying, my custody is illegal under 4001. The United States would come back and say, sorry but it's authorized under 5003, as a defensive matter.

turns.name: EASTON
turns.text: What would happen is, I think we'd get in that position that Justice Marshall suggested earlier, is that we would have the prisoner trying to wander back and forth corrections philosophy, so that those who need the entitlements which Vermont statutes give them, if offered by the federal system, we can take advantage of that. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Very well. Mr. Nelson, do you have anything further?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: Just a few points, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think Vermont can't make some other entity a surrogate, including a private corporation? Suppose some group of people got together, penologists in the universi­ was passed to permit incarceration in the federal system as a reciprocal act to 5003. If Vermont were able without such a statute to designate another agent to incarcerate its prisoners, then the Vermont reciprocal statute would not have been neces­ sary . I want to address the agency interpretation argument which was presented by the Solicitor General. In the first place, we think that the agency's interpretation is entitled to weight only if it's reasonable. In this case we don't think contained in the Code of Federal Regulations, and it simply tracks the statutory language. It does not omit the treatment facilities and personnel requirement.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, couldn't special cases mean Alcatraz?

turns.name: NELSON
turns.text: It could, but it was, the Deputy Attor­ ney General said that it mostly related to juveniles, and I think he was referring to the YCA, which had been passed in the previous Congress, that the 82nd Congress made the YCA available to the states, as well as other treatment facilities.
